item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has been already reported soon after this article's publication, but the seasonal dates are still off and ambiguous.

During the seasons of winter and spring (18th of December to 18th of March)

These are winter's date of start and end, minus four to six days. I am ready to accept that the SCP effects start before the equinoxes and solstices, because there is no reason why they couldn't.
However, it should read instead "During the seasons of winter and spring (18th of December to 18th of June)" to make sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had not thought of this, real nice idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, the female pregnancies are more or less just an extension of the "stuff grows" effect, but the SCiP actually reproduces by knockin' up the dudes, whose pregnancies are supposed to end with the he-mom's rotting corpse fertilizing the plant-fetus (treetus?), which presumably grows into a new leaf-bro.

I mean, The thing could probably make the men sprout vaginas if it wanted them to live.

Or am I just being Captain Obvious?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The image in this article was removed because we could not properly determine whether or not the image was properly licensed or attributed. It may be added once again if/when proper attribution or proper use under CC guidelines is confirmed. Please contact anqxyr or a member of the Image Team if you have questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure why people think the impregnation thing is weird - clearly, this creature is…well, for lack of a better word, a nature god, and during the spring, it helps all living things with growing and producing new life; human impregnation is simply what it does to us while it helps everything grow. Even the impregnation of males makes sense - this creature affects all living things individually, and males aren't able to grow new humans inside their bodies without modification, and so the effect modifies them.

Everything else about this SCP on top of that makes me feel like this deserves a much higher rating. Of course, maybe I'm just a lame, tree-hugging hippie. Still, upvoted hard, and I think I might call this my new favorite SCP! :D

There is one thing that did make me raise my eyebrows, though: The picture. It's a green sketch of a person and some vines - it doesn't even resemble SCP-1010. Just saying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a good SCiP! Right now, there are a couple of little quibbles I have with the presentation (image doesn't quite match the description, pregnancy in males causing internal hemorrhaging? Even though they have a womb made for them?) but the idea is solid enough to withstand this.

Like I said before, I like monsters, as long as they're interesting enough. This one is.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To me, the males becoming implanted with the thing's embryo was kinda cool. Think about it: if the kids produced by the women are normal humans but the ones produced by men are genetically identical to SCP-1010, then this thing must've been the result of a successful male implantation

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one. It's polite and creepy at the same time.
I enjoyed the part about it imprenating people. It makes the SCP more believable somehow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting idea that just doesn't work for me as an SCP. Requires too much "A wizard did it"-type handwaving to shoehorn the required elements in, sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If a male was to form an intact uterus, then he should not develop hemorrhaging. It would be more likely that he would bleed to death at the start of labor, since there would be no vagina or other opening that the fetus could pass through.

After birth, does a hysterectomized woman keep the new uterus?

Otherwise, I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think so too, however, I suggest this fact be made a little more believable and serious looking by giving a reason for this, for example, the fertilized ovum to be placed in a structure like the peritoneum, or somehow a spore or ovum make it's way to the testicles or any way to contact spermatozoids and therefore become fertilized so that a fetus can be formed. Obviously, birthing could only be accomplished by caesarian surgery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The 'males becoming pregnant' bit just seems tacked on to me, and doesn't add much to the article besides a cheap ick factor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, but in truth it could use more work.
I'm sorry I don't have any suggestions to make it better
I'll up vote it, but I'd like to see this one develop some more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did have the days of the month for each season's beginnings and ends, but people in the chat pointed out there was equinoxes and solistaces so I just had it as the seasons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Winter is about 21 Dec through 21 Mar, and spring is about 21 Mar through 21 Jun (plus or minus a day on each end). Also, you have gallons of water where you should have liters, and several run-on sentences that I might fix at some point. The concept itself I'm ambivalent over, so no vote at the moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I also liked the last addendum. One correction: "The rate of change from one species of leaf to another takes an average of 10 seconds, how this is achieved is unknown." should be "The rate of change from one species of leaf to another takes an average of 10 seconds; how this is achieved is unknown."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the last addendum, but the rest of it didn't interest me- not sure why.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's the green man.

'nuff said.

I'm gonna refrain from voting, as it just doesn't do anything for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh yeah, forgot to thank Salmander and several other people for their help with it while I was writing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "It is to be noted that females that have had a hysterectomy are still subject to becoming pregnant, as a complete uterus is grown as well as the fertilised ovum."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if the woman has had a hysterectomy?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I think of anything cool, I'll chuck that in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted. But as I said in chat, I would still like to see you do more with this. Mooooooore.
